video_title: GitHub’s Agent Era: 14x Commits, 200M Developers, Copilot’s Next Act — Kyle Daigle
gist: GitHub COO (and now Microsoft CMO of Developer) Kyle Daigle quantifies an agent-driven explosion in developer activity — 200M+ GitHub accounts and a run-rate of ~14B commits/year (14x YoY) — that is straining GitHub's core infrastructure and forcing a shift to Azure compute. He frames Copilot's next act as agent orchestration plus a context layer (Work IQ / Foundry IQ), positions Microsoft's OpenClaw and Windows/Azure sandboxing investment as building the 'OS for AI agents,' and argues the binding constraints are now CPU compute and human/enterprise trust rather than model quality.

insights.claim: GitHub now has over 200 million developer accounts, up from roughly 80 million previously, driven largely by non-traditional users entering software in the AI era.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest, GitHub COO / Microsoft CMO of Developer)
insights.so_what: A ~2.5x jump in the addressable developer base is the top-line TAM expansion for every AI-coding tool vendor; watch whether monetization keeps pace with account growth.
insights.quote: Oh, we're over 200 million now.

insights.claim: GitHub did about 1 billion commits in all of 2025 but is now running at 275 million commits per week, putting it on pace for ~14 billion in 2026 — roughly 14x year-on-year growth that is still accelerating.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: 14x growth in core platform activity is the clearest quantitative proxy for AI-agent coding adoption; it signals surging downstream demand for CI/CD compute and storage.
insights.quote: 1 billion commits in 2025. Now it's 275 million per week on pace for 14 billion this year.

insights.claim: GitHub is now doing more activity (PRs, commits, by nearly every measure) in a single month than it did in an entire year previously, breaking systems in novel rather than familiar ways.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Step-function load growth implies sustained capex on compute and infra rewrites; a leading indicator for Microsoft/Azure cloud-spend allocation toward developer workloads.
insights.quote: now we're doing more in a month than we did in a year last year.

insights.claim: Daigle predicts the growth multiple will keep compounding beyond 14x toward 30x or 50x, framing current scaling work as preparation for the next exponential wave.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Management is sizing infrastructure for order-of-magnitude further growth, signaling continued heavy infra investment rather than a plateau.
insights.quote: if it's not 14, then it's 30 or it's 50 or whatever the next, you know, exponential growth is going to be.

insights.claim: The surge in repos hitting milestones like 100,000 GitHub stars at record speed reflects a swelling cohort of new, non-professional builders rather than purely organic developer demand or only bots.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Star velocity is being inflated by audience expansion and gaming alike, eroding stars as a trust/quality signal investors or acquirers use to gauge OSS traction.

insights.claim: GitHub's recent reliability outages stem from agent-driven growth breaking assumptions baked into 10–15-year-old services, forcing rewrites rather than simple capacity adds; vertical and horizontal scaling no longer suffice.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Signals a multi-quarter infra-rewrite cycle at GitHub; reliability risk could pressure enterprise renewals if outages persist, but management claims availability is already improving.
insights.quote: vertical doesn't really work anymore. horizontal isn't work either because like we're all we all have some CPU or GPU constraints in the world now

insights.claim: GitHub is expanding beyond its own data centers to add Azure cloud compute specifically because agent-driven builds have made CPU (not just GPU) capacity a binding constraint for GitHub Actions.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Concrete intra-Microsoft compute shift to Azure; CPU scarcity as a named bottleneck is a non-obvious signal for the broader compute-supply thesis beyond GPUs.
insights.quote: now CPUs are becoming a factor

insights.claim: GitHub is using a new Azure 'dev compute' service under the hood to spin up small VMs and containers very fast for sandboxed tool calls and cloud agents.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific Azure primitive monetizing the agent-sandboxing trend; a building block competing with offerings like E2B/Modal/Daytona.

insights.claim: GitHub's most persistent scaling pain is in its permissioning layer, much of which still sits in a legacy database internally called 'MySQL 1' that the team has been incrementally decomposing for years.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the specific technical debt behind cross-the-board outages; resolution timeline is gated by database physics, not quick fixes.
insights.quote: many of our permissioning layers sit into a database that we like internally call my SQL 1.

insights.claim: A structural shift back toward large monorepos (reversing years of many-small-repos) is a major new source of git-infrastructure strain, since big repos with large blobs have unique performance problems.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Monorepo resurgence reshapes git infra investment priorities; relevant to code-hosting and build-tooling vendors optimizing for large-repo performance.
insights.quote: Repos are bigger and there's uh not fewer of them

insights.claim: GitHub multiplies its scaling difficulty by also running its full stack on-prem via GitHub Enterprise Server and in data-resident single-location setups, each with distinct storage/permissioning characteristics that contributed to broad outages.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: On-prem and data-residency commitments add cost and fragility; a structural constraint on how fast GitHub can re-architect for the agent era.

insights.claim: Daigle expects GitHub availability incidents to decline materially over the next three months as foundational rewrites land, characterizing recent weeks as already better than prior periods.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A near-term reliability inflection is the watch item for enterprise-retention risk; track GitHub status/incident frequency through Q3 2026.
insights.quote: each month I expect you know in the next 3 months you're going to see like fewer and fewer moments where we have an availability problem

insights.claim: GitHub Copilot has grown from ~100 million to roughly 300 million in a headline metric (likely users/seats reach), per recent figures Daigle cites loosely, with dollar amounts not disclosed.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Even hedged, a ~3x Copilot metric jump frames Microsoft's lead in AI-coding distribution; the undisclosed revenue is the missing variable for valuation.
insights.quote: I think the last number I think Mario came to my conference uh and talked about the hundred million mark. I think most recently 300.

insights.claim: Copilot lost momentum because GitHub spent ~1.5 years after code-completion investing heavily in fine-tuning (both a shared model and per-customer fine-tunes) just as a new generation of frontier models 'sherlocked' that work and a wave of rival AI coding tools emerged.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A candid admission that betting on fine-tuning over agent harnesses ceded ground; explains the competitive opening Cursor and others exploited.
insights.quote: the models got better. And so then ever since we've been really on this kind of journey

insights.claim: GitHub has consolidated Copilot onto a single underlying SDK and agent harness powering the coding agent, a new CLI, a new desktop app, and cloud agents, with task fan-out ('fleet') and autopilot-style multiplexing.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A unified harness across surfaces is Microsoft's structural counter to point-solution rivals; the desktop app is now Daigle's 'daily driver,' signaling the strategic surface.
insights.quote: a single underlying uh SDK and harness for our uh our uh coding agent

insights.claim: Copilot supports bring-your-own-key and use of all models, while GitHub continues to post-train its own language-specific models for code completion.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Model-agnosticism plus owned models hedges Microsoft against any single model vendor; relevant to OpenAI/Anthropic dependence questions.

insights.claim: Daigle argues the AI-coding category is not at 'end of history' despite agents covering the full SDLC, because agents remain myopic — losing all non-coding context for security/governance reasons — leaving a large opportunity in ambient, context-rich AI.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames the next competitive battleground as context/ambient AI rather than raw code-gen, reshaping where incumbents and startups should invest.
insights.quote: We're all still in such this like hyper myopic era of AI

themes.theme: Context layer as the next moat (Work IQ / Foundry IQ)

insights.claim: Microsoft's Work IQ MCP server and Foundry IQ are enterprise context engines that let agents query across M365 work assets (and existing data stores via Foundry IQ) without moving data or leaking private information, which Daigle calls the unlock that lets agents work safely at large companies.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Positions a governed enterprise context layer as Microsoft's durable moat over standalone coding tools; the named products to watch at Build.
insights.quote: if you are in the M365 land in any way, check out work IQ, check out Foundry IQ. These little like uh uh oversimplifying it context engines are wild good

insights.claim: Daigle's central thesis is that software development was never a single-lane coding task — the real value is connecting all context (spec docs, email, Slack, transcripts, online conversations) into the agent's decision-making, and no current tool does this well.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Defines the gap (context aggregation) as the next product frontier; whoever solves governed cross-source context captures the enterprise agent market.
insights.quote: I think the most interesting thing to me in AI is actual ambient AI

insights.claim: Daigle reveals that the Work IQ context teams and the Copilot teams have effectively been merging work over recent months, since outside of code assets everything reduces to a shared context problem.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Org-level integration signals Microsoft is unifying coding agents with its enterprise context stack — a structural advantage in bundling.
insights.quote: that's literally what has been happening the last several months

themes.theme: OpenClaw & the 'OS for AI agents' bet

insights.claim: Microsoft has appointed a Corporate VP dedicated to OpenClaw despite not owning it, because OpenClaw personifies a valuable agent that has access to all of a user's information and can operate a computer — the model Microsoft wants to enable safely on Windows at work.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A CVP-level commitment to an external OSS agent signals where Microsoft sees the platform fight; track Build announcements on Windows agent sandboxing.
insights.quote: Microsoft has a CVP. Dedicated to OpenClaw.

insights.claim: Microsoft is investing in OS-level sandboxing on Windows and cloud sandboxing on Azure so that personal/work agents can run on a work device without security risk ('use that claw and not get fired'), reframing the operating system for an era where non-humans use it.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Microsoft is leveraging its OS incumbency to become the safe substrate for agents — the 'OS for AI' positioning; a key differentiator vs. pure-cloud rivals.
insights.quote: operating systems need to look different than they looked 5 years ago because it's not just you using them anymore.

insights.claim: Microsoft teams contribute substantial full-time engineering into open-source agent projects like OpenClaw while deliberately avoiding public credit, reflecting a strategy of supplying platform components rather than only shipping a single vertical product.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Microsoft's 'build for builders' component strategy aims to own the agent platform layer; relevant to how value accrues to platforms vs. app-layer startups.

insights.claim: Daigle argues the binding compute question is shifting from 'we need more inference' to 'what type of inference and compute do we need' to run agentic flows, a multi-layer problem reaching down to Azure silicon.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes compute demand as heterogeneous (agent workloads differ from chat inference), informing how to read Azure capex and silicon-design priorities.
insights.quote: It's not just okay, we need more inference. It's okay, well, what type of inference do we need?

insights.claim: npm now has far more underlying usage than at acquisition and faces a structural security dilemma: nearly every move to make it more secure (2FA changes, token rotation, manifest work) breaks large numbers of dependent developers.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: npm is critical internet infrastructure with an inherent security-vs-compatibility tension; supply-chain risk here is systemic to the JS ecosystem and its dependents.
insights.quote: every move that we make to make it more secure will break a lot of people.

insights.claim: Daigle is skeptical that 'slot forks' / AI-vendoring of source code (per Vercel's Malte) eliminates supply-chain vulnerabilities, because agents can be tricked many ways into trusting insecure code; ongoing static analysis and runtime testing remain necessary.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Tempers hype that AI vendoring solves OSS security; sustains demand for code-scanning vendors (GitHub Advanced Security, Socket) rather than displacing them.
insights.quote: I don't think it will sol

insights.claim: Daigle frames trust in agent-generated PRs as fundamentally a social/human problem, not solvable by verification tooling alone, and says existing GitHub signals (stars, commit counts) are passive and gameable rather than active trust signals.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies opportunity in building durable identity/trust primitives for agent contributions — a gap GitHub itself flags as unsolved and possibly a future product or human-digital-ID play.
insights.quote: it's a it's a human problem to review it

insights.claim: GitHub deliberately waits for community/RFC consensus before cementing standards (as it did with the pull request) rather than imposing security processes, citing fragmented maintainer needs — one maintainer's vouch system fails for others.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Explains GitHub's slow, opt-in standardization posture; a structural constraint on how quickly platform-level security can be enforced across OSS.
insights.quote: We usually wait for the sort of like RFC process socially

insights.claim: GitHub envisions configurable, agentic trust heuristics (e.g., GitHub Actions workflows that auto-reject PRs based on contributor history, account age, social-handle linkage) rather than imposing one global standard, while warning every fixed rule is gameable by patient attackers.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Points to where GitHub may extend its platform to monetize/standardize agent-PR trust controls; an adjacency for security-tooling vendors.

themes.theme: GitHub Actions as compute engine & monetization

insights.claim: GitHub Actions is the core compute layer behind the outages because more tools, agents and PRs mean more builds and thus more CPU demand; it is also a meaningful but entitlement-heavy revenue line, with many minutes given away as part of entitlements.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Actions sits at the intersection of cost (CPU) and revenue; the give-away entitlement model means agent-driven build growth could pressure margins absent pricing changes.
insights.quote: more tools more agents more PRs mean more builds. more builds need more CPUs

insights.claim: Developers increasingly use GitHub Actions not just for CI/CD but for general processing, automation and agentic workflows, broadening (and stressing) its role as a general-purpose compute platform.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: Actions creeping into general compute expands its strategic value but also its cost exposure; a usage pattern to watch for future metered pricing.

themes.theme: AI in enterprise operations & labor reshaping

insights.claim: GitHub deployed AI internally to ~3,000 employees by distributing a shared CLI plus micro 'skills' that read across GitHub, email and Slack, deliberately avoiding new tools so non-technical staff never had to change how they work.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A concrete enterprise-AI rollout pattern (CLI + composable skills over existing data) that other large orgs may emulate; bullish for context/MCP tooling, bearish for net-new AI SaaS point tools.
insights.quote: no one has to change how they work

insights.claim: Daigle argues the era of large monolithic 'mega skills' is ending in favor of tiny single-purpose 'micro skills' composed like Legos, because mega skills become unmaintainable as workflows change.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A design-pattern shift relevant to agent-tooling and MCP vendors; favors composable, atomic capabilities over monolithic packaged workflows.
insights.quote: we're ending the era of these like massive beautiful perfect skills

insights.claim: Daigle built an entire revenue-planning presentation end-to-end with AI (using a SQLite app, Obsidian notes and skills) and presented it to the CRO and CFO without anyone detecting it was AI-generated, even crafting it to look deliberately non-AI.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A real example of AI displacing knowledge-work deliverables (slide/spreadsheet prep) at executive level; supports the thesis that white-collar task automation is already operational, not speculative.
insights.quote: never came up once it was AI generated.

insights.claim: Daigle argues AI doesn't eliminate roles like chief of staff but reshapes them — away from building slides toward higher-value human-connection and relationship-mapping work.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: A measured take on AI labor impact (task displacement, role persistence) useful for calibrating white-collar productivity and headcount theses.

insights.claim: Daigle contends former developers now in leadership are uniquely advantaged in the AI era because pattern-recognition and problem-solving skills plus the ability to read/edit code in plain English let them wield agents more effectively than non-technical peers.
insights.speaker: Kyle Daigle (guest)
insights.so_what: An implied view on which human capital appreciates as agents proliferate; relevant to talent and org-design theses in AI-native companies.
insights.quote: I can crank up 15 agents on Saturday
